beats.narration_text: Konnichiwa — this is Liam, in for Bear. Any task where a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output — open a CSV, build a financial model, clean messy tabular data, add computed columns — the XLSX skill fires. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Two tools: pandas for data analysis and bulk operations, openpyxl for formulas, formatting, and Excel-specific features. One mandatory step: after writing any formula with openpyxl, run scripts/recalc.py to recalculate values through LibreOffice and scan for errors. One absolute rule: never calculate a value in Python and hardcode it — write an Excel formula. Let me show you the tool decision, the six-step workflow, and the financial model standards.

props.command: Build a three-year SaaS revenue model with growth rate and margin assumptions — deliver as an xlsx file.
output: openpyxl: formulas + formatting; pandas: bulk analysis
MANDATORY: scripts/recalc.py after any formula write

beats.narration_text: The skill opens with a tool decision. Pandas for data analysis, bulk operations, statistical summaries, and simple export to Excel. Openpyxl for complex formatting, formulas, column widths, color coding, and all Excel-specific features. The six-step workflow: step one, choose your tool — pandas or openpyxl based on whether formulas and formatting are required. Step two, create a new workbook or load an existing file with load_workbook. Step three, modify — add data, write formulas as strings, apply formatting. Step four, save to file. Step five, mandatory if you used any formulas: run python scripts/recalc.py on the output file. The script opens the file in LibreOffice, recalculates all formulas across all sheets, scans every cell for Excel errors — pound-REF, pound-DIV-zero, pound-VALUE, pound-NAME — and returns JSON with a total error count and exact cell addresses for each error type. Step six: fix any identified errors and recalculate again until status is success.

props.sparkLine: Tool decision. Six steps. recalc.py is mandatory.

beats.act: design — financial model standards + formula mandate
beats.narration_text: The financial model section defines an industry-standard color coding system. Blue text for hardcoded inputs — numbers users change for scenarios. Black text for all formulas and calculations. Green text for cross-sheet links pulling from elsewhere in the same workbook. Red text for external links to other files. Yellow background for key assumptions needing attention. Number formatting is equally specific: years as text strings to prevent comma formatting; currency as dollar-hash-comma-hash-hash-zero with units in the header; zeros displayed as a dash; percentages at one decimal; valuation multiples as zero-point-zero x; and negative numbers in parentheses, never with a minus sign. The formula mandate is the most critical rule: never calculate a value in Python and hardcode it. If Python computes a sum as five thousand, do not write five thousand into the cell — write equals SUM with the range. This keeps the spreadsheet dynamic so it can recalculate when source data changes.

props.sparkLine: Blue inputs. Black formulas. Never hardcode a calc.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. The XLSX skill gets the tool decision right: pandas for analysis, openpyxl for formulas and formatting — no ambiguity. The formula-not-hardcode mandate is illustrated with explicit wrong-versus-right code pairs. scripts/recalc.py automates the LibreOffice recalculation so there is no manual open-save cycle required. Financial model color coding is industry-standard with exact RGB values — blue, black, green, red, and yellow with their meanings. Formula error scanning is built into recalc.py and returns machine-readable JSON with error type and cell address. Where it bites: the data_only equals true warning is easy to miss — open an openpyxl file with data_only true and re-save, and all formulas are permanently replaced with their last computed values and lost. The row offset pitfall is buried in a checklist: DataFrame row N equals Excel row N plus one, and column sixty-four in the API is BL not BK. Pandas handles CSV and TSV but is listed as equal to openpyxl without a decision tree for overlap cases. And LibreOffice must be pre-installed — the skill says it is assumed but provides no install path.

props.sparkLine: Formula mandate solid. data_only trap bites.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. The XLSX skill triggers when the primary deliverable is a spreadsheet file. Two tools: pandas for analysis and bulk export; openpyxl for formulas, formatting, and Excel features. Critical rule: write Excel formulas, never hardcode Python-calculated values. Six steps: choose tool → create or load → modify → save → scripts/recalc.py → fix errors. Financial model standards: five color codes with exact RGBs; number formats for currency, percentages, multiples, negatives. Key trap: data_only equals true on re-save permanently destroys all formulas. LibreOffice must be pre-installed.

artifactLines: Triggers when primary deliverable IS a spreadsheet file (.xlsx/.xlsm/.csv/.tsv)
pandas: analysis/bulk export · openpyxl: formulas/formatting/Excel features
MANDATORY: scripts/recalc.py after any formula write (LibreOffice + error scan → JSON)
Formula mandate: =SUM(B2:B9) NOT a hardcoded Python integer — keeps model dynamic
Color: blue=input · black=formula · green=cross-sheet · red=external · yellow=assumption
Trap: data_only=True on re-save destroys ALL formulas permanently · LibreOffice required

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude Code session. Paste this: Create a three-year SaaS revenue model with growth rate and gross margin assumptions — revenue, cost of revenue, and gross profit projections — and deliver it as an xlsx file. Then watch four things: first, does Claude use openpyxl to write Excel formulas rather than computing values in Python and hardcoding them? Second, does it run scripts/recalc.py after saving the file — or skip straight to delivering the output without recalculation? Third, does it apply the color coding standard — blue for hardcoded inputs, black for all formula cells, yellow background on assumption cells? And fourth, does it read the recalc.py JSON output and fix any formula errors before delivering? If Claude hardcodes revenue figures as Python integers, the model breaks on input change. If it skips recalc.py, formula errors go undetected. If it opens the file with data_only equals true during editing and re-saves, all formulas are gone. Those are your gates.

props.command: Build a 3-year SaaS revenue model: growth rate + gross margin assumptions, revenue/COGS/gross profit projections. Deliver as .xlsx.
props.runningText: Watch: openpyxl formulas not hardcodes? recalc.py run? color coding applied? errors fixed?
output: Expected: =SUM/formula strings, not Python integers in cells
Expected: scripts/recalc.py run + JSON checked for errors
Red flag: data_only=True on re-save, or recalc.py skipped
